title: Two-time interval method to circumvent particle image velocimetry dynamic range
author: Bharadwaj, Vybhav

steps.title: The method: dual-time intervals and a normalized median test
steps.instruction: A typical PIV system uses a pulsed laser that illuminates the flow field at desired times synchronised with the camera. A pulsed laser consists of two lasers sharing a laser head, with the time-interval between two laser pulses, Δt, being as low as a few micro-second (μs); the laser itself has a frequency anywhere between 10 Hz and a few kHz. The PIV camera captures a series of images corresponding to the timing diagram shown inFig. 2 in a single run: here two different time intervals arise when the PIV-analysis is performed on images 1 and 2 and 1 and 3 for which the time-intervals are denoted by Δt1 and Δt2, respectively, with Δt2 > Δt1.Fig. 1Flow chart of the algorithm. The symbols are referred from the main article. Subscripts “s” and “l” refer to short and long-time intervals respectively.Fig 1:Fig. 2Timing diagram for the pulsed laser: Δt1 = 1ms and Δt2 = 100ms.Fig 2:

steps.title: The method: dual-time intervals and a normalized median test
steps.instruction: Every instant of the flow field analysed using PIV results in M × N vectors. A 3 × 3 or 5 × 5 window-size is selected, and the median is calculated.(1)Um=Median(U1,U2,...,Un),where n = (Window size)2, and this procedure is repeated for every vector cell. The residual for each vector, ri, and its median, rm, are calculated as follows:(2)ri=|Ui−Um|rm=Median(ri)where i = 1: n. Here, rm is the median residual which we further normalise with Um (the median of the original dataset),(3)r′=rm/Um which is dubbed the “normalised median residual”. This quantity (3) will be used to identify spurious vectors.
